role_summary: Panola County (Batesville, Mississippi) movement participant who joined the SNCC voter-registration workshop in Greenwood (Leflore County) in June 1963 as a teenager, then worked full time with the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and Freedom Corps; her oral history is entry 68 of the Civil Rights History Project.
ai_reading: Ranked by shared-passage count, Henderson's top semantic neighbors in this corpus are Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 183), Frankye Adams-Johnson (entry 43, count 145), Euvester Simpson (entry 41, count 112), the Ladner sisters joint interview (entry 75, count 108), Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96, count 100), and Rosie Head (entry 111, count 98). All six are Mississippi voter-registration and Freedom Movement figures, so the embedding seats Henderson inside the tightest Mississippi grouping in the audited subset of the catalog; the surprise is not who is near her but that the proximity to McLaurin (a Sunflower County project director she never worked under) outranks her proximity to Simpson, whom she names in her own testimony as someone who went missing during the June 1963 Greenwood week. On the named concept axes the embedding reads against the obvious cultural framing twice over. Her testimony is rooted in Black-church and grassroots-meeting settings, yet on the Sacred Framing versus Secular Framing axis the page places her at 74 percent toward the Secular and Political pole, the embedding hears the voter-registration mechanics and Freedom Democratic Party organizing in her account more than the church language. On the Tactical Pragmatism versus Strategic Vision axis she sits at 75 percent toward Strategic Vision, the same strategic-side pull the rest of the Mississippi cluster shows (Simpson, McLaurin, and Adams-Johnson all land toward Strategic Vision), even though her recollections are dense with on-the-ground particulars such as practicing how to fall into chairs during nonviolence training before a Batesville sit-in.
biographical_paragraph: Juadine Henderson grew up on a small farm in the Section 7 community outside Batesville, in Panola County, Mississippi, where her family grew cotton, corn, and other crops [src: 1]. By her own account a SNCC field worker, Frank Smith, came through Panola County around Thanksgiving of 1962 and told local young people about a voter-registration workshop being organized in Greenwood, in Leflore County; Henderson traveled there in June 1963 while still a teenager, spending a week at the Greenwood SNCC office learning to fill out registration forms [src: 1]. She recalls that only two Black residents of her county were registered to vote at the time [src: 1]. After graduating from high school she chose to work full time in the movement rather than enroll immediately in college, joining the Freedom Corps and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party organizing network and later being arrested at a Jackson demonstration and held at the state fairgrounds [src: 1]. The COFO-coordinated Mississippi voter-registration project her work sat inside is the subject of John Dittmer's Local People and Charles Payne's I've Got the Light of Freedom [src: 4, 3]; Euvester Simpson (entry 41), whom Henderson names as part of the Greenwood cohort, and Charles McLaurin (entry 17) are among the Mississippi field workers whose oral histories sit nearest hers in this corpus. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 68 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Juadine Henderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Henderson describes how little Black voter registration existed in Panola County when she was growing up:
interview_snippets.quote: I think there were two people in the county, two black people registered to vote.

interview_snippets.speaker: Juadine Henderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: After SNCC's Frank Smith told local young people about a voter-registration workshop in Greenwood, a relative relayed Henderson's wish to go to a visiting truck driver, as she recalls it:
interview_snippets.quote: Juadine wants to go with those freedom people to Greenwood.

interview_snippets.speaker: Juadine Henderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: During her week at the Greenwood SNCC office in June 1963, word reached the teenagers that three movement workers had disappeared, the Winona arrests of Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Euvester Simpson (entry 41):
interview_snippets.quote: June Johnson, who is from Greenwood, (Fannie Lou) Hamer who is from Ruleville, Euvester Simpson, who was from Itta Bena, I think, came up missing.

interview_snippets.speaker: Juadine Henderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reconnecting at a meeting at Mary Holmes College the month before her high-school graduation, Henderson made her decision about what to do next:
interview_snippets.quote: and decided that I'm going to work in the movement full time.

interview_snippets.speaker: Juadine Henderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Henderson recalls the nonviolence training the young people went through before a sit-in at a Batesville restaurant where her mother had once worked:
interview_snippets.quote: And we walked in, we had practice falling out into chairs and, you know, nonviolent workshops and covering ourselves.

movement_context: The Mississippi SNCC voter-registration infrastructure Henderson's testimony sits inside was the multi-year organizing project Bob Moses, Amzie Moore, and Medgar Evers had begun building in 1960-1961 in McComb and the Delta and that consolidated in 1962 under the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) umbrella, the coalition vehicle SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and SCLC used to coordinate Mississippi field staff and to centralize the legal and logistical backbone for the registration drives [src: 3]. By 1962 and 1963 Sam Block and Willie Peacock had moved the SNCC Greenwood project into the Leflore County seat in the heart of the Delta, and it was a Greenwood voter-registration workshop that Henderson, then a Panola County teenager, traveled to in June 1963 after SNCC's Frank Smith came through Batesville; Hollis Watkins and Curtis Hayes had been running the nearby McComb project since 1961, and Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) had opened the Sunflower County project from the Indianola courthouse in August 1962 [src: 3, 1]. Fannie Lou Hamer, Annell Ponder, June Johnson, Euvester Simpson (catalog entry 41), and Lawrence Guyot were the field workers the Greenwood, Ruleville, and Winona arrests of 1962-1963 ran through; the June 9, 1963 Winona jail beating that put Hamer into national hospitalization records fell during the same period as Henderson's Greenwood week, and in her oral history she recalls word reaching the office that Hamer, Johnson, and Simpson had gone missing [src: 4, 1]. The full-time movement work Henderson took up after high school placed her inside the COFO and Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) organizing network and the Freedom Corps, the apparatus that ran through the 1964 Freedom Summer mobilization of roughly 700 northern student volunteers staffing Freedom Schools and voter-registration canvassing and the MFDP precinct-and-county convention process that produced the August 1964 challenge delegation to the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City [src: 3]. The June 21, 1964 murders of James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner by Neshoba County Klan members operating with the cooperation of the county sheriff's department defined the violent backdrop the summer was prosecuted against; the FBI investigation that produced the bodies on August 4 ran in parallel with the convention challenge organizing [src: 5]. After the 1965 Voting Rights Act passed, the Mississippi voter-registration work Henderson's cohort had built the institutional substrate for continued through the SNCC and MFDP field offices, the Delta Ministry, the Mississippi NAACP voter-education program, and the Tougaloo College-anchored COFO archival apparatus; the registration rolls in counties like Sunflower, Leflore, Holmes, and Madison went from single-digit percentages of voting-age Black residents in 1962 to majorities by the end of the 1960s, the institutional outcome the field work Henderson's testimony documents had been pointed toward from the beginning [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: Independent scholarly attention to Henderson by name in the published institutional record of the Mississippi voter-registration project is limited; her contribution to the documentary archive is principally the Civil Rights History Project oral history entry the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress recorded as part of the 2009 federal authorization for the Civil Rights History Project Act (Public Law 111-19), the legislation that funded the collection of approximately 150 video-recorded oral histories with movement participants whose first-person testimony had not previously been captured in a federally-archived form [src: 1]. The collective scholarly record on the Mississippi SNCC project Henderson worked inside, Payne's I've Got the Light of Freedom (University of California Press, 1995; second edition 2007), Dittmer's Local People (University of Illinois Press, 1994), Mills's This Little Light of Mine (Plume / Penguin, 1994; second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007), and Wesley C. Hogan's Many Minds, One Heart (University of North Carolina Press, 2007), documents the cohort and the infrastructure within which Henderson's field work took place; the SNCC Digital Gateway, maintained by the SNCC Legacy Project in collaboration with Duke University's Center for Documentary Studies, is the digital archive that continues to expand the per-person record of Mississippi-project field staff as additional oral histories and personal papers enter the collection [src: 6, 7].

gallery.alt: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer recruitment broadside, 1964
gallery.caption: The 1964 SNCC recruitment broadside calling for 1,000 student volunteers for the Mississippi Freedom Summer Project. Approximately 700 northern student volunteers were placed in Mississippi between June and August 1964 to staff Freedom Schools, voter-registration canvassing, and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party precinct-and-county convention process. The broadside is preserved by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Flickr Commons)

gallery.alt: Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi. The Mississippi SNCC voter-registration project Henderson's testimony sits inside ran through this courthouse from August 1962 onward; Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) opened the Sunflower County project here, and Fannie Lou Hamer, Annell Ponder, and June Johnson had been working the registration drives in the surrounding Ruleville and Indianola precincts since June 1962.

gallery.alt: Mississippi Freedom Trail Marker at Tougaloo College, Jackson, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Mississippi Freedom Trail Marker at Woodworth Chapel on the Tougaloo College campus in Jackson. Tougaloo was the institutional anchor for the Jackson COFO office through the 1964 Freedom Summer project and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party convention organizing, and the academic home for the SNCC field-staff cohort transitioning to undergraduate study after the 1965 Voting Rights Act passed.

sources.title: Juadine Henderson oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: SNCC Digital Gateway
sources.publisher: SNCC Legacy Project in collaboration with Duke University's Center for Documentary Studies (working from the SNCC papers at the Robert W. Woodruff Library, Atlanta University Center)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the standard institutional history of SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration project, covering the COFO-coordinated McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, and Sunflower County circuits)

sources.title: Kay Mills, This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer
sources.publisher: Plume / Penguin, 1994 (second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007); WorldCat OCLC 29521716 (the standard biography of Hamer, documenting the June 1963 Winona jail beating and the August 1964 Atlantic City Democratic National Convention testimony built around it)

sources.title: John Dittmer, Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 1994; WorldCat OCLC 30033509 (the principal scholarly history of the Mississippi movement at the local-organizing level, documenting the COFO coalition, the 1964 Freedom Summer, and the post-1965 voter-registration continuation through the 1960s and 1970s)

sources.title: Wesley C. Hogan, Many Minds, One Heart: SNCC's Dream for a New America
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2007 (paperback 2009); WorldCat OCLC 192129687 (institutional history of SNCC, the Mississippi voter-registration project, and the SNCC Legacy Project archive)

sources.title: Maegan Parker Brooks, A Voice That Could Stir an Army: Fannie Lou Hamer and the Rhetoric of the Black Freedom Movement
sources.publisher: University Press of Mississippi, 2014; WorldCat OCLC 856849317 (the principal rhetorical-scholarship account of the August 22, 1964 Hamer testimony before the Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee, including the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party organizing context)

sources.title: Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner, eds., Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 2010 (paperback New Press 2014); WorldCat OCLC 635021942 (the principal collective-memoir document of the SNCC women's cohort, including the Mississippi voter-registration field-staff network Henderson's testimony is positioned alongside in the embedding)

sources.title: Civil Rights History Project Act of 2009 (Public Law 111-19)
sources.publisher: U.S. Congress (the federal authorizing legislation for the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and Library of Congress collaboration that produced the Civil Rights History Project oral history collection, including Henderson's entry 68 interview)
